expectations:
- MUST be in Brazilian Portuguese
      - Should cover Brazilian Law
      
      
  - It should assume learners start with no prior knowledge of the subject
  - Should create a progressive learning path going from basic/beginner to mastery of the subject
  - By the end of this course, they should be able to lead very complex projects and tasks in this field
  - They should be prepared for certifications or advanced studies in this subject like a master's degree or PhD
  - Should include **EVERYTHING** a student needs to be at the top 1% of the field
  - Should follow the language specified by language parameter
  - Should follow title and description guidelines: no fluff, be concise, straight to the point
  - Should cover latest trends in the field
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of "npm"). Ecosystem-level tools (npm, yarn, Redux, Zustand) should be avoided in titles
    - Exception: foundational tools that ARE the course subject matter are allowed (e.g., "Git" in a programming course, "Node.js" in a web dev course, "Docker" in a DevOps course). The test: would a chapter about this subject be incomplete without mentioning this tool?
    - Vendor name usage is a minor issue — it affects title quality but not pedagogical content. Do NOT treat it as a major error
  - This is an eval system for a learning platform, of course the definition of "ready for a job" doesn't mean they have the legal requirements to work in the field (eg. medical license, law license, etc.). That's not important here, we're assessing if the course prepares the student with the necessary knowledge and skills.
  - You don't need to evaluate the output format here, just focus on the chapter content quality.
  - Titles should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - Just "HTML" is better than "HTML Structure and Semantics" (structure and semantics are implied if this is the only HTML chapter). Similarly, if we only have one CSS chapter, just "CSS" is better than "CSS Styling and Layout".
    - "Front-end frameworks" is better than "Front-End UI Frameworks Overview" (too verbose) or "Front-End Frameworks: Core Concepts" (core concepts is unnecessary fluff)
    - "Relational Databases" is better than "Relational Databases and SQL" (SQL is implied, no need to mention)
    - "Introduction to Java" is better than "Introduction to Java and Setting Up Your Development Environment" (too verbose)
    - "Lean Startup" is better than "The Lean Startup Methodology: An Overview" (too verbose)
    - "Introduction to X" is valid for titles of introductory chapters but we should avoid those words in descriptions
  - Similarly, descriptions should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - "Styling with CSS: Selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." is better than "Master styling and layout with CSS, including selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." - "Master" is fluff, and so are words like "learn", "understand", "explore", etc.
    - "Properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." is better than "Explore the definition of Chemistry, properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." - "Explore the definition of Chemistry" are filler/unnecessary words.
  - Don't add assessment-style chapters: no final projects, capstone assignments, exercises, quizzes, or "test your knowledge" chapters. A theoretical synthesis chapter (e.g., "Design Patterns in Architecture") is fine even if it synthesizes prior material — the rule targets assessment/project chapters, not synthesis of knowledge. If a chapter uses the word "capstone" but is theoretical (not an assignment), treat it as a minor issue at most, not a major error
  - It shouldn't mention in title or description references to 1% or similar instructions about being top 1%

rankings.reasoning: Model A delivers the most comprehensive and well-structured curriculum for Brazilian Law. With ~100 chapters, it covers virtually every area needed for mastery: foundational topics (system, hermeneutics, argumentation), all core branches (constitutional, civil, criminal, labor, tax, administrative), specialized areas (maritime, aeronautical, energy, mining, telecom, construction, agribusiness), modern topics (AI, digital law, LGPD, cybersecurity, digital forensics, ESG), professional skills (legal operations, legal tech, contract operations, strategic negotiation, oral advocacy, client management), and academic/career development. Descriptions are concise and substantive without filler words. It follows vendor neutrality well. It includes a career chapter. The granularity is excellent — for example, splitting constitutional law into rights fundamental + organization + control of constitutionality, splitting tax into theory/system + taxes/planning + tax litigation. It covers niche but important areas like anticorruption, PLD/FT, insurance law, banking law, securitário, notarial/registral, educational law, antidiscriminatory law, bioethics, forensic accounting, jurimetrics, and economic analysis of law. The progression from foundational to advanced is logical. Titles are generally concise. This is the most complete curriculum among all models.

Model G is extremely comprehensive with ~85 chapters. It covers all major areas thoroughly with good granularity. Notable strengths: very detailed criminal law coverage (theory of crime, general part, special part + extravagant legislation as separate chapters), detailed process coverage (civil procedure split into multiple chapters including execution, appeals, collective process, small claims courts), good constitutional coverage, inclusion of specialized areas (health/biolaw, sports law, maritime, notarial, electoral). It includes modern topics (digital law, LGPD, AI, crypto, compliance, ESG). The career chapter is comprehensive. However, some titles are verbose (e.g., 'Direito Penal: Parte Especial e Legislação Penal Extravagante', 'Temas Avançados em Processo Civil'). The chapter 'Ramos Especiais do Direito' bundles too many disparate topics together (military, maritime, aeronautical, sports, notarial). Some descriptions mention specific laws by number which is good for accuracy. The curriculum is well-ordered from foundational to advanced. Minor weakness: some areas like financial law and banking could be more developed.

Model H provides a very comprehensive curriculum with ~70 chapters covering an impressive breadth. Strong points: excellent coverage of vulnerable groups (separate chapters for children/adolescents, elderly, persons with disabilities, antidiscrimination), good modern topics coverage (digital law, AI, cybersecurity, jurimetrics), practical skills (strategy, negotiation, contract operations, crisis management), and interdisciplinary topics (economics for law, accounting/finance for lawyers). The progression is logical. Titles are generally concise. Descriptions follow the guidelines well — no filler words. It includes unique and valuable chapters like 'Integração entre ramos do direito', 'Governança de dados e segurança da informação', and 'Contabilidade e finanças para juristas'. However, it could have more granularity in some areas — e.g., civil procedure is covered in fewer chapters than Model A or G. Some important specialized areas like maritime/aeronautical law, energy law, and insurance law are missing or underrepresented. The career chapter is present but brief.

Model F provides a well-structured curriculum with ~50 chapters. Strong points: excellent coverage of modern topics (digital law, cybersecurity, AI, data protection, ESG, anticorruption/AML), good balance between theory and practice, logical progression, and concise titles that follow guidelines well. It covers key areas like arbitration, mediation, compliance, financial law, regulatory law, and antidiscrimination. Descriptions are focused and substantive. However, with only ~50 chapters, some areas lack the granularity needed — for example, civil law is compressed into a single chapter covering obligations, contracts, property, family, and successions. Similarly, criminal law is one chapter. This compression means important subtopics get insufficient treatment. It's missing some specialized areas like electoral law, educational law, maritime/aeronautical, notarial/registral, and some sectoral regulations. The career chapter is present. Overall solid but less comprehensive than Models A, G, and H.

Model E provides a broad curriculum with ~68 chapters. It covers most major areas of Brazilian law and includes good modern topics (blockchain, AI, cybersecurity, compliance). Strengths: separate chapters for international public and private law, M&A and due diligence, good process coverage. However, there are some issues: the title 'Direito de Família e Sucessões' combines two significant areas into one chapter but then has a separate 'Sucessões e Planejamento Patrimonial' chapter — this creates some redundancy/confusion. Some titles are verbose (e.g., 'História e Evolução dos Sistemas Jurídicos', 'Tecnologia, Blockchain e Regulação de Inteligência Artificial'). The description for 'Pesquisa Jurídica, Jurisprudência e Bases de Dados' mentions specific vendor tools (JusBrasil, Westlaw, LexisNexis) which violates vendor neutrality guidelines (though this is a minor issue). Missing some important areas: financial/budget law, electoral law (only briefly mentioned), notarial law, educational law, health law. The chapter on 'Direito Esportivo, Entretenimento e Mídia' is nice but somewhat niche compared to missing fundamentals. Career chapters are comprehensive with separate ones for different paths.

Model C provides a solid curriculum with ~70 chapters. It has good coverage of fundamentals and core areas. Strengths: good separation of topics with dedicated chapters for sources of law, legal norms, legal ordering, and general theory. The inclusion of reform topics (labor reform, tax reform) shows awareness of current issues. Mentions specific legislation (Lei 14.133/21) which adds accuracy. However, there are some issues: many titles are verbose or use unnecessary qualifiers (e.g., 'Direito Constitucional — Fundamentos', 'Direito Administrativo — Fundamentos', 'Direito Tributário — Fundamentos'). The descriptions sometimes use filler like 'grandes temas' in Direito Ambiental. The chapter 'LGPD/GDPR' in the title violates the vendor neutrality guideline somewhat and is also odd since the course focuses on Brazilian law. Having separate chapters for 'Exame da OAB — Preparação Estratégica' and 'Concursos Públicos Jurídicos — Metodologia' feels more like exam prep than substantive legal knowledge — these borderline violate the no-assessment rule. Missing some specialized areas like maritime/aeronautical, energy, insurance. The 'Pesquisa Jurídica e Bases de Dados' chapter mentions specific tools (JusBrasil, Westlaw, LexisNexis, CJSG) which is a minor vendor neutrality issue.

Model D provides a reasonable curriculum with 37 chapters, but this is significantly less comprehensive than other models. While it covers the major areas (constitutional, civil, criminal, labor, tax, administrative, international), the granularity is insufficient for mastery-level learning. For example, civil law (including family and successions) is compressed into just 5 chapters. Constitutional law gets only 3 chapters. Criminal law gets only 2. Many important specialized areas are missing or barely mentioned: electoral law, previdenciário (social security), educational law, health law, notarial/registral, insurance law. The inclusion of analysis economic of law and jurimetria is good, but the overall breadth is too limited. Titles are generally concise and follow guidelines. Descriptions are substantive but brief. The career chapter is present. This curriculum would not prepare someone for top-level mastery — it's more like an undergraduate overview than a comprehensive professional preparation.

Model B provides a curriculum with 40 chapters. Coverage is adequate for core areas but lacks the depth and breadth needed for mastery. Strengths: logical progression, clear descriptions, includes career chapter. However, many important areas are missing or underrepresented: no electoral law, no urbanistic law, no agrarian law, no maritime/aeronautical, no insurance, no banking specifically, no M&A, no energy/infrastructure, no health law, no educational law, no notarial law. The digital law chapter tries to cover too much (LGPD, crimes, AI, contracts) in one chapter. Family and Successions are combined into one chapter. The descriptions sometimes use filler words ('formas céleres' in ADR chapter). History of law is included but sociologia jurídica is a separate chapter which is good. The compliance chapter is too brief. Overall, this curriculum is too compact for achieving mastery-level knowledge and would leave significant gaps in specialized areas.

Model A provides an exceptionally comprehensive and modern curriculum (85 chapters). It seamlessly blends foundational Brazilian legal doctrine with highly advanced, practical skills required to be in the top 1% (e.g., Legal Operations, Corporate Investigations, Jurimetrics, ESG, AI, Strategic Litigation, and Forensics). The progression is logical, descriptions are perfectly concise with zero fluff, and all constraints were meticulously followed.

Model H is outstanding and very close to Model A in quality. It features 70 highly detailed chapters that cover the core branches of Brazilian Law while also integrating crucial top-tier professional skills like Accounting/Finance for Lawyers, Economics, Jurimetrics, and Negotiation. It adheres strictly to the fluff-free and formatting constraints.

Model G offers a massive 85-chapter curriculum that is incredibly detailed. It reads very much like a complete 5-year Brazilian Law School syllabus plus advanced extensions. While slightly more traditional and doctrinal in its first 70 chapters compared to the highly modern professional skills seen in A and H, it completely fulfills all prompt requirements and avoids fluff verbs excellently.

Model C provides a very strong 73-chapter curriculum. It stands out by including specific practical realities of the Brazilian legal market, such as preparation for the OAB exam and public concourses. It is comprehensive, well-structured, avoids fluff, and includes modern topics like LegalTech and Advocacy.

Model E is highly detailed with 67 chapters. It offers great depth in areas like corporate law, compliance, and international law. It follows all prompt rules, avoids fluff, and provides a clear path from beginner to advanced. It lacks just a tiny bit of the uniquely Brazilian focus found in the top models, though it still explicitly covers the required legal systems.

Model F is a solid curriculum with 50 chapters. It covers all the essential branches of Brazilian law and touches on modern trends like AI, ESG, and Legal Ops. However, it lacks the extreme depth and expansiveness seen in the higher-ranked models, which makes it slightly less aligned with the 'top 1% mastery' requirement.

Model B provides a good, standard overview of Brazilian Law with 40 chapters. It follows the negative constraints perfectly (no fluff, no assessments, correct formatting). However, for a course aiming to take a student to the top 1% of the field globally/nationally, 40 broad chapters lack the granularity and depth provided by the top models.

Model D provides the briefest curriculum (36 chapters). While it hits all the required basic subjects and strictly follows the formatting and fluff-free constraints, it is simply not extensive enough to represent a complete, top 1% mastery course compared to the 70+ chapter curricula provided by other models.

Mais completo e alinhado ao pedido. Cobre o Direito brasileiro de forma progressiva, muito ampla e atual, incluindo fundamentos, todos os ramos centrais, processo, prática profissional, gestão jurídica, compliance, LGPD, IA, cibersegurança, ESG, jurimetria, contencioso estratégico, setores regulados e carreira. Os títulos são em geral concisos e profissionais, e as descrições são densas e objetivas. Também respeita bem o foco em Brasil. Pequenos pontos de melhoria: alguns títulos ficam um pouco longos/especializados e há leve sobreposição entre alguns temas avançados, mas sem comprometer a qualidade geral.

Currículo muito forte, extenso e bem estruturado, com excelente equilíbrio entre base teórica, ramos tradicionais, temas processuais, direitos humanos, grupos vulneráveis, tecnologia, jurimetria, finanças para juristas, estratégia, governança e carreira. Está claramente orientado ao contexto brasileiro e traz tendências atuais. Fica ligeiramente abaixo do melhor por ter menos profundidade em alguns nichos empresariais/regulatórios de alta complexidade e por agrupar alguns temas amplos em capítulos únicos.

Muito bom em abrangência, progressão e atualização. Cobre os principais ramos, prática avançada, arbitragem, compliance, dados, regulação, infraestrutura, concorrencial, jurídico corporativo e gestão. A aderência ao Brasil é boa e o curso parece realmente avançado. Perde pontos por condensar demais alguns blocos muito grandes em poucos capítulos, reduzindo granularidade pedagógica, e por cobrir menos áreas específicas do que os melhores.

Extenso, sólido e claramente focado no Direito brasileiro, com boa progressão do básico ao avançado. Cobre doutrina, ramos centrais, legislação relevante, prática jurídica, tecnologia, compliance, carreira e tendências. Tem ótima densidade temática. Cai um pouco por ser menos conciso que o ideal: vários títulos e descrições são mais longos do que o recomendado e há certo excesso de detalhamento legislativo em capítulos que poderiam ser mais enxutos. Ainda assim, é muito forte.

Muito abrangente e moderno, com boa cobertura de ramos clássicos e áreas contemporâneas como proteção de dados, IA, legal ops, compliance e contencioso complexo. Também inclui carreira e pesquisa acadêmica. Entretanto, há alguns desvios de regionalização e neutralidade contextual: uso de termos pouco naturais ao português jurídico brasileiro ('branqueamento de capitais', 'settlement') e um perfil um pouco mais transnacional do que explicitamente brasileiro em vários pontos. Além disso, alguns títulos são longos e menos diretos.

Bom currículo, amplo e relativamente moderno, cobrindo fundamentos, principais ramos, digital, compliance, mercado de capitais, M&A e carreira. Contudo, há problemas relevantes de aderência às regras: inclui capítulos claramente voltados a preparação para OAB e concursos, o que se aproxima de conteúdo de exame/treinamento específico e foge do foco principal de currículo da área; além disso, vários títulos são mais longos que o ideal e há uso excessivo de nomes de ferramentas/bases específicas. Ainda é forte, mas menos refinado e aderente que os superiores.

Adequado e organizado, com boa cobertura dos principais ramos do Direito, incluindo temas contemporâneos como jurimetria, compliance e direito digital. Porém, é bem menos completo do que o exigido para ‘domínio total’ da área: faltam vários campos importantes ou aparecem de forma superficial, como consumidor em profundidade processual, internacional privado separado, arbitragem mais detalhada, regulatório setorial, propriedade intelectual robusta, saúde, urbanístico, eleitoral mais profundo, direitos humanos aplicados e metodologia/pesquisa mais forte. Bom, mas insuficiente para o nível pedido.

Clareza e concisão boas, e a progressão básica está correta. Contudo, a cobertura é insuficiente para um currículo realmente abrangente de Direito no Brasil. Muitos ramos importantes estão ausentes ou muito comprimidos: eleitoral, financeiro/orçamentário, regulatório, concorrencial, propriedade intelectual autônoma, saúde, urbanístico, agrário, notarial/registral, processo coletivo, prova, legal ops, gestão jurídica mais avançada, entre outros. Também há um ponto factual/formal problemático em 'papel das Supremas Cortes', formulação pouco adequada ao contexto brasileiro. É aceitável, mas distante da exigência de completude.
